title: Cuban picadillo
subtitle: Beef mince braised with sofrito, olives, raisins, and capers — the everyday family dinner of Havana kitchens.
excerpt: Beef mince browned hard and then braised with onion, pepper, garlic, tomato, olives, capers, and raisins. The sweet-salty-savoury balance is the dish; served over white rice with sliced ripe plantain. Twenty-five minutes, weeknight-easy, freezer-friendly.
sourceNotes: Picadillo (from picar, to mince) is the Spanish-Caribbean ground-meat braise that varies country to country. The Cuban version is distinctive for its addition of raisins, which give the sweetness that balances the brininess of the olives and capers. Versions exist across Mexico, Puerto Rico, and the Philippines, each with regional ingredients. The Cuban version dates to the colonial Spanish period and has been the everyday family dinner for at least 150 years.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes very well for up to three months. The flavours deepen after freezing.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles easily. A natural weekly batch for the freezer.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Tastes better on day two. Reheats gently with a splash of stock.

content.text: The trick with picadillo is to brown the mince hard. Most home cooks dump the mince into a pan and stir it as it cooks, which steams the meat and gives a grey, watery result. The Cuban method spreads the mince thinly across a hot pan, leaves it undisturbed for 4 minutes to develop a brown crust, then breaks it up and continues. That crust is where the flavour lives.

The raisins divide opinion. Cuban cooks insist on them; their job is to balance the salty olives and capers with a counterweight of sweetness. The dish tastes flatter without them. If you really dislike them, double the dried oregano and add a splash of sweet sherry instead.
A wide, heavy frying pan or a sauté pan with a lid. Steamed white rice, black beans, and slices of fried sweet plantain to serve.

Warm {{olive-oil}} of oil in a wide frying pan over a medium-high heat. Add {{beef-mince}} of beef mince and spread it across the base of the pan in a thin layer. Leave it undisturbed for 4 minutes; the underside should develop a dark brown crust. Break it up with a wooden spoon and stir for another 2 minutes until thoroughly browned. The pan base should look caramelised, not grey.

Push the mince to one side of the pan. Add the chopped {{onion}} onion and {{pepper-green}} green pepper to the empty side. Cook for 5 minutes, stirring just the vegetables, until softened. Stir in {{garlic}} of crushed garlic, {{cumin-ground}} of cumin, and {{oregano-dried}} of oregano. Now mix everything together.

Stir in {{tomato-puree}} of tomato purée and cook for one minute. Pour in {{tinned-tomatoes}} of chopped tomatoes and {{stock-beef}} of hot stock. Add {{olives-green}} of halved olives, {{capers}} of capers, {{raisins}} of raisins, {{bay-leaves}} of bay, and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Bring to a gentle simmer.

Simmer uncovered for 15 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the sauce has thickened and the oil starts to separate at the edges. Fish out the bay leaves. Taste and adjust the salt — the olives and capers carry a lot, so go gently. Serve over white rice with a side of black beans and slices of fried plantain.

items.cause: Pan was not hot enough, or mince was stirred too soon.
items.fix: Drain the liquid and continue cooking on a higher heat for 5 more minutes to brown the meat. Next time, leave the mince undisturbed for the full 4 minutes.

items.cause: Olives and capers brought enough salt without the extra teaspoon.
items.fix: Add a splash of red wine vinegar and another handful of raisins to rebalance. Hold the added salt during the season step next time.

items.cause: Too many raisins for the salt level, or sauce reduced past the right point.
items.fix: Add an extra tablespoon of capers and a squeeze of lemon. The dish wants sweet, salty, and savoury in balance; if one dominates, top up the other two.

items.cause: Pan too narrow to encourage evaporation, or too much liquid.
items.fix: Transfer to a wider pan and continue reducing. The 30 cm frying pan is the right tool; a narrower pan steams the sauce rather than reducing it.

content.text: For picadillo a la mexicana, swap the olives, capers, and raisins for diced potatoes and chopped jalapeños; the dish becomes a different one. For a Puerto Rican leaning, add a tablespoon of sofrito (the green herb paste, sold in Caribbean shops) at the same time as the garlic. Use turkey mince for a lighter version that keeps the technique intact.

Picadillo is the most Tuesday-night dish in the Cuban canon. It assembles fast, uses cupboard ingredients, and the leftovers fill empanadas, top tacos, or stuff peppers. The Cuban version with raisins is the one most cooks meet first; from there the dish has dozens of regional cousins across Latin America. Worth keeping the recipe close because it solves the question of what to do with a kilo of mince better than almost anything.
